title: Detect a brute-force source
prompt: Write detect_bruteforce(lines, threshold). Each failed-login line looks like 'Failed password for <user> from <ip> port <n>'. Return a sorted list of the source IPs whose failure count is GREATER THAN threshold.

tests.call: detect_bruteforce(['Failed password for root from 1.2.3.4 port 22','Failed password for x from 1.2.3.4 port 22','Failed password for y from 1.2.3.4 port 22','Failed password for z from 5.6.7.8 port 22'], 2)
detect_bruteforce(['Failed password for root from 1.2.3.4 port 22','Failed password for x from 1.2.3.4 port 22','Failed password for y from 1.2.3.4 port 22'], 3)

help.plain: Count failed logins per source IP, and return the IPs that failed more than the threshold number of times.
help.analogy: Like flagging the one door someone jiggled fifty times — repeated failures from one place is the tell.
help.mnemonic: Count per IP, keep the ones OVER the line (strictly greater than threshold).
help.nudge: Build a dict of ip -> count. The IP is the word right after 'from'. Then keep IPs where count is strictly greater than threshold (not equal).

details.restate: Find brute-force sources in auth-log lines: count failed attempts per source IP, and return (sorted) the IPs that exceeded the threshold. 'Exceeded' means strictly greater than, so exactly threshold does NOT count.

io.because: 3 failures is greater than the threshold of 2
3 is not greater than 3 — the threshold is a strict cutoff

terms.meaning: an attacker trying many passwords rapidly — shows up as many failed logins from one source
